Newer versions (magic versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records\\n32:  * regardless of compression.\\n33:  */\\n34: public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable<Record> {\\n35: \\n36: Either way, callers should ensure that the iterator is closed.\\n233:      *\\n234:      * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression if supported.\\n235:      *                                    For small record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4)\\n236:      *                                    will dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating over the records in the\\n237:      *                                    batch. {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01V6MJNCV4PexhKT4nhrydet","type":"tool_result","content":"{\"content\":\"1: /*\\n2:  * Licensed to the Apache Software Foundation (ASF) under one or more\\n3:  * contributor license agreements. See the NOTICE file distributed with\\n4:  * this work for additional information regarding copyright ownership.\\n5:  * The ASF licenses this file to You under the Apache License, Version 2.0\\n6:  * (the \\\"License\\\"); you may not use this file except in compliance with\\n7:  * the License. You may obtain a copy of the License at\\n8:  *\\n9:  *    http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0\\n10:  *\\n11:  * Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software\\n12:  * distributed under the License is distributed on an \\\"AS IS\\\" BASIS,\\n13:  * WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.\\n14:  * See the License for the specific language governing permissions and\\n15:  * limitations under the License.\\n16:  */\\n17: package org.apache.kafka.common.record.internal;\\n18: \\n19: import org.apache.kafka.common.InvalidRecordException;\\n20: import org.apache.kafka.common.KafkaException;\\n21: import org.apache.kafka.common.compress.Compression;\\n22: import org.apache.kafka.common.errors.CorruptRecordException;\\n23: import org.apache.kafka.common.header.Header;\\n24: import org.apache.kafka.common.record.TimestampType;\\n25: import org.apache.kafka.common.utils.BufferSupplier;\\n26: import org.apache.kafka.common.utils.ByteBufferOutputStream;\\n27: import org.apache.kafka.common.utils.ByteUtils;\\n28: import org.apache.kafka.common.utils.CloseableIterator;\\n29: import org.apache.kafka.common.utils.Crc32C;\\n30: \\n31: import java.io.IOException;\\n32: import java.io.InputStream;\\n33: import java.nio.BufferUnderflowException;\\n34: import java.nio.ByteBuffer;\\n35: import java.util.ArrayList;\\n36: import java.util.Collections;\\n37: import java.util.Iterator;\\n38: import java.util.List;\\n39: import java.util.NoSuchElementException;\\n40: import java.util.Objects;\\n41: import java.util.OptionalLong;\\n42: \\n43: import static org.apache.kafka.common.record.internal.Records.LOG_OVERHEAD;\\n44: \\n45: /**\\n46:  * RecordBatch implementation for magic 2 and above. The schema is given below:\\n47:  *\\n48:  * RecordBatch =>\\n49:  *  BaseOffset => Int64\\n50:  *  Length => Int32\\n51:  *  PartitionLeaderEpoch => Int32\\n52:  *  Magic => Int8\\n53:  *  CRC => Uint32\\n54:  *  Attributes => Int16\\n55:  *  LastOffsetDelta => Int32 // also serves as LastSequenceDelta\\n56:  *  BaseTimestamp => Int64\\n57:  *  MaxTimestamp => Int64\\n58:  *  ProducerId => Int64\\n59:  *  ProducerEpoch => Int16\\n60:  *  BaseSequence => Int32\\n61:  *  RecordsCount => Int32\\n62:  *  Records => [Record]\\n63:  *\\n64:  * Note that when compression is enabled (see attributes below), the compressed record data is serialized\\n65:  * directly following the count of the number of records.\\n66:  *\\n67:  * The CRC covers the data from the attributes to the end of the batch (i.e. all the bytes that follow the CRC). It is\\n68:  * located after the magic byte, which means that clients must parse the magic byte before deciding how to interpret\\n69:  * the bytes between the batch length and the magic byte. The partition leader epoch field is not included in the CRC\\n70:  * computation to avoid the need to recompute the CRC when this field is assigned for every batch that is received by\\n71:  * the broker. The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial is used for the computation.\\n72:  *\\n73:  * On Compaction: Unlike the older message formats, magic v2 and above preserves the first and last offset/sequence\\n74:  * numbers from the original batch when the log is cleaned. This is required in order to be able to restore the\\n75:  * producer's state when the log is reloaded. If we did not retain the last sequence number, then following\\n76:  * a partition leader failure, once the new leader has rebuilt the producer state from the log, the next sequence\\n77:  * expected number would no longer be in sync with what was written by the client. This would cause an\\n78:  * unexpected OutOfOrderSequence error, which is typically fatal. The base sequence number must be preserved for\\n79:  * duplicate checking: the broker checks incoming Produce requests for duplicates by verifying that the first and\\n80:  * last sequence numbers of the incoming batch match the last from that producer.\\n81:  *\\n82:  * Note that if all of the records in a batch are removed during compaction, the broker may still retain an empty\\n83:  * batch header in order to preserve the producer sequence information as described above. These empty batches\\n84:  * are retained only until either a new sequence number is written by the corresponding producer or the producerId\\n85:  * is expired from lack of activity.\\n86:  *\\n87:  * There is no similar need to preserve the timestamp from the original batch after compaction. The BaseTimestamp\\n88:  * field therefore reflects the timestamp of the first record in the batch in most cases. If the batch is empty, the\\n89:  * BaseTimestamp will be set to -1 (NO_TIMESTAMP). If the delete horizon flag is set to 1, the BaseTimestamp\\n90:  * will be set to the time at which tombstone records and aborted transaction markers in the batch should be removed.\\n91:  *\\n92:  * Similarly, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the maximum timestamp of the current records if the timestamp type\\n93:  * is CREATE_TIME. For LOG_APPEND_TIME, on the other hand, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the timestamp set\\n94:  * by the broker and is preserved after compaction. Additionally, the MaxTimestamp of an empty batch always retains\\n95:  * the previous value prior to becoming empty.\\n96:  *\\n97:  * The current attributes are given below:\\n98:  *\\n99:  *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n100:  *  | Unused (7-15) | Delete Horizon Flag (6) | Control (5) | Transactional (4) | Timestamp Type (3) | Compression Type (0-2) |\\n101:  *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n102:  */\\n103: public class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch implements MutableRecordBatch {\\n104:     static final int BASE_OFFSET_OFFSET = 0;\\n105:     static final int BASE_OFFSET_LENGTH = 8;\\n106:     static final int LENGTH_OFFSET = BASE_OFFSET_OFFSET + BASE_OFFSET_LENGTH;\\n107:     static final int LENGTH_LENGTH = 4;\\n108:     static final int PARTITION_LEADER_EPOCH_OFFSET = LENGTH_OFFSET + LENGTH_LENGTH;\\n109:     static final int PARTITION_LEADER_EPOCH_LENGTH = 4;\\n110:     static final int MAGIC_OFFSET = PARTITION_LEADER_EPOCH_OFFSET + PARTITION_LEADER_EPOCH_LENGTH;\\n111:     static final int MAGIC_LENGTH = 1;\\n112:     public static final int CRC_OFFSET = MAGIC_OFFSET + MAGIC_LENGTH;\\n113:     static final int CRC_LENGTH = 4;\\n114:     static final int ATTRIBUTES_OFFSET = CRC_OFFSET + CRC_LENGTH;\\n115:     static final int ATTRIBUTE_LENGTH = 2;\\n116:     public static final int LAST_OFFSET_DELTA_OFFSET = ATTRIBUTES_OFFSET + ATTRIBUTE_LENGTH;\\n117:     static final int LAST_OFFSET_DELTA_LENGTH = 4;\\n118:     static final int BASE_TIMESTAMP_OFFSET = LAST_OFFSET_DELTA_OFFSET + LAST_OFFSET_DELTA_LENGTH;\\n119:     static final int BASE_TIMESTAMP_LENGTH = 8;\\n120:     static final int MAX_TIMESTAMP_OFFSET = BASE_TIMESTAMP_OFFSET + BASE_TIMESTAMP_LENGTH;\\n121:     static final int MAX_TIMESTAMP_LENGTH = 8;\\n122:     static final int PRODUCER_ID_OFFSET = MAX_TIMESTAMP_OFFSET + MAX_TIMESTAMP_LENGTH;\\n123:     static final int PRODUCER_ID_LENGTH = 8;\\n124:     static final int PRODUCER_EPOCH_OFFSET = PRODUCER_ID_OFFSET + PRODUCER_ID_LENGTH;\\n125:     static final int PRODUCER_EPOCH_LENGTH = 2;\\n126:     static final int BASE_SEQUENCE_OFFSET = PRODUCER_EPOCH_OFFSET + PRODUCER_EPOCH_LENGTH;\\n127:     static final int BASE_SEQUENCE_LENGTH = 4;\\n128:     public static final int RECORDS_COUNT_OFFSET = BASE_SEQUENCE_OFFSET + BASE_SEQUENCE_LENGTH;\\n129:     static final int RECORDS_COUNT_LENGTH = 4;\\n130:     static final int RECORDS_OFFSET = RECORDS_COUNT_OFFSET + RECORDS_COUNT_LENGTH;\\n131:     public static final int RECORD_BATCH_OVERHEAD = RECORDS_OFFSET;\\n132: \\n133:     private static final byte COMPRESSION_CODEC_MASK = 0x07;\\n134:     private static final byte TRANSACTIONAL_FLAG_MASK = 0x10;\\n135:     private static final int CONTROL_FLAG_MASK = 0x20;\\n136:     private static final byte DELETE_HORIZON_FLAG_MASK = 0x40;\\n137:     private static final byte TIMESTAMP_TYPE_MASK = 0x08;\\n138: \\n139:     private final ByteBuffer buffer;\\n140: \\n141:     DefaultRecordBatch(ByteBuffer buffer) {\\n142:         this.buffer = buffer;\\n143:     }\\n144: \\n145:     @Override\\n146:     public byte magic() {\\n147:         return buffer.get(MAGIC_OFFSET);\\n148:     }\\n149: \\n150:     @Override\\n151:     public void ensureValid() {\\n152:         if (sizeInBytes() < RECORD_BATCH_OVERHEAD)\\n153:             throw new CorruptRecordException(\\\"Record batch is corrupt (the size \\\" + sizeInBytes() +\\n154:                     \\\" is smaller than the minimum allowed overhead \\\" + RECORD_BATCH_OVERHEAD + \\\")\\\");\\n155: \\n156:         if (!isValid())\\n157:             throw new CorruptRecordException(\\\"Record is corrupt (stored crc = \\\" + checksum()\\n158:                     + \\\", computed crc = \\\" + computeChecksum() + \\\")\\\");\\n159:     }\\n160: \\n161:     /**\\n162:      * Gets the base timestamp of the batch which is used to calculate the record timestamps from the deltas.\\n163:      *\\n164:      * @return The base timestamp\\n165: The schema is given below:\\n47:  *\\n48:  * RecordBatch =>\\n49:  *  BaseOffset => Int64\\n50:  *  Length => Int32\\n51:  *  PartitionLeaderEpoch => Int32\\n52:  *  Magic => Int8\\n53:  *  CRC => Uint32\\n54:  *  Attributes => Int16\\n55:  *  LastOffsetDelta => Int32 // also serves as LastSequenceDelta\\n56:  *  BaseTimestamp => Int64\\n57:  *  MaxTimestamp => Int64\\n58:  *  ProducerId => Int64\\n59:  *  ProducerEpoch => Int16\\n60:  *  BaseSequence => Int32\\n61:  *  RecordsCount => Int32\\n62:  *  Records => [Record]\\n63:  *\\n64:  * Note that when compression is enabled (see attributes below), the compressed record data is serialized\\n65:  * directly following the count of the number of records.\\n66:  *\\n67:  * The CRC covers the data from the attributes to the end of the batch (i.e. all the bytes that follow the CRC). It is\\n68:  * located after the magic byte, which means that clients must parse the magic byte before deciding how to interpret\\n69:  * the bytes between the batch length and the magic byte. The partition leader epoch field is not included in the CRC\\n70:  * computation to avoid the need to recompute the CRC when this field is assigned for every batch that is received by\\n71:  * the broker. The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial is used for the computation.\\n72:  *\\n73:  * On Compaction: Unlike the older message formats, magic v2 and above preserves the first and last offset/sequence\\n74:  * numbers from the original batch when the log is cleaned. This is required in order to be able to restore the\\n75:  * producer's state when the log is reloaded. If we did not retain the last sequence number, then following\\n76:  * a partition leader failure, once the new leader has rebuilt the producer state from the log, the next sequence\\n77:  * expected number would no longer be in sync with what was written by the client. This would cause an\\n78:  * unexpected OutOfOrderSequence error, which is typically fatal. The base sequence number must be preserved for\\n79:  * duplicate checking: the broker checks incoming Produce requests for duplicates by verifying that the first and\\n80:  * last sequence numbers of the incoming batch match the last from that producer.\\n81:  *\\n82:  * Note that if all of the records in a batch are removed during compaction, the broker may still retain an empty\\n83:  * batch header in order to preserve the producer sequence information as described above. These empty batches\\n84:  * are retained only until either a new sequence number is written by the corresponding producer or the producerId\\n85:  * is expired from lack of activity.\\n86:  *\\n87:  * There is no similar need to preserve the timestamp from the original batch after compaction. The BaseTimestamp\\n88:  * field therefore reflects the timestamp of the first record in the batch in most cases. If the batch is empty, the\\n89:  * BaseTimestamp will be set to -1 (NO_TIMESTAMP). If the delete horizon flag is set to 1, the BaseTimestamp\\n90:  * will be set to the time at which tombstone records and aborted transaction markers in the batch should be removed.\\n91:  *\\n92:  * Similarly, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the maximum timestamp of the current records if the timestamp type\\n93:  * is CREATE_TIME. For LOG_APPEND_TIME, on the other hand, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the timestamp set\\n94:  * by the broker and is preserved after compaction. Additionally, the MaxTimestamp of an empty batch always retains\\n95:  * the previous value prior to becoming empty.\\n96:  *\\n97:  * The current attributes are given below:\\n98:  *\\n99:  *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n100:  *  | Unused (7-15) | Delete Horizon Flag (6) | Control (5) | Transactional (4) | Timestamp Type (3) | Compression Type (0-2) |\\n101:  *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n102:  */\\n103: public class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch implements MutableRecordBatch {\\n104:     static final int BASE_OFFSET_OFFSET = 0;\\n105:     static final int BASE_OFFSET_LENGTH = 8;\\n106:     static final int LENGTH_OFFSET = 1;\\n112:     public static final int CRC_OFFSET = MAGIC_OFFSET + MAGIC_LENGTH;\\n113:     static final int CRC_LENGTH = 4;\\n114:     static final int ATTRIBUTES_OFFSET = CRC_OFFSET + CRC_LENGTH;\\n115:     static final int ATTRIBUTE_LENGTH = 2;\\n116:     public static final int LAST_OFFSET_DELTA_OFFSET = ATTRIBUTES_OFFSET + ATTRIBUTE_LENGTH;\\n117:     static final int LAST_OFFSET_DELTA_LENGTH = 4;\\n118:     static final int BASE_TIMESTAMP_OFFSET = LAST_OFFSET_DELTA_OFFSET + LAST_OFFSET_DELTA_LENGTH;\\n119:     static final int BASE_TIMESTAMP_LENGTH = 8;\\n120:     static final int MAX_TIMESTAMP_OFFSET = BASE_TIMESTAMP_OFFSET + BASE_TIMESTAMP_LENGTH;\\n121:     static final int MAX_TIMESTAMP_LENGTH = 8;\\n122:     static final int PRODUCER_ID_OFFSET = MAX_TIMESTAMP_OFFSET + MAX_TIMESTAMP_LENGTH;\\n123:     static final int PRODUCER_ID_LENGTH = 8;\\n124:     static final int PRODUCER_EPOCH_OFFSET = PRODUCER_ID_OFFSET + PRODUCER_ID_LENGTH;\\n125:     static final int PRODUCER_EPOCH_LENGTH = 2;\\n126:     static final int BASE_SEQUENCE_OFFSET = PRODUCER_EPOCH_OFFSET + PRODUCER_EPOCH_LENGTH;\\n127:     static final int BASE_SEQUENCE_LENGTH = 4;\\n128:     public static final int RECORDS_COUNT_OFFSET = BASE_SEQUENCE_OFFSET + BASE_SEQUENCE_LENGTH;\\n129:     static final int RECORDS_COUNT_LENGTH = 4;\\n130:     static final int RECORDS_OFFSET = RECORDS_COUNT_OFFSET + RECORDS_COUNT_LENGTH;\\n131:     public static final int RECORD_BATCH_OVERHEAD = RECORDS_OFFSET;\\n132: \\n133:     private static final byte COMPRESSION_CODEC_MASK = 0x07;\\n134:     private static final byte TRANSACTIONAL_FLAG_MASK = 0x10;\\n135:     private static final int CONTROL_FLAG_MASK = 0x20;\\n136:     private static final byte The schema is given below:\\n47:  *\\n48:  * RecordBatch =>\\n49:  *  BaseOffset => Int64\\n50:  *  Length => Int32\\n51:  *  PartitionLeaderEpoch => Int32\\n52:  *  Magic => Int8\\n53:  *  CRC => Uint32\\n54:  *  Attributes => Int16\\n55:  *  LastOffsetDelta => Int32 // also serves as LastSequenceDelta\\n56:  *  BaseTimestamp => Int64\\n57:  *  MaxTimestamp => Int64\\n58:  *  ProducerId => Int64\\n59:  *  ProducerEpoch => Int16\\n60:  *  BaseSequence => Int32\\n61:  *  RecordsCount => Int32\\n62:  *  Records => [Record]\\n63:  *\\n64:  * Note that when compression is enabled (see attributes below), the compressed record data is serialized\\n65:  * directly following the count of the number of records.\\n66:  *\\n67:  * The CRC covers the data from the attributes to the end of the batch (i.e. all the bytes that follow the CRC). It is\\n68:  * located after the magic byte, which means that clients must parse the magic byte before deciding how to interpret\\n69:  * the bytes between the batch length and the magic byte. The partition leader epoch field is not included in the CRC\\n70:  * computation to avoid the need to recompute the CRC when this field is assigned for every batch that is received by\\n71:  * the broker. The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial is used for the computation.\\n72:  *\\n73:  * On Compaction: Unlike the older message formats, magic v2 and above preserves the first and last offset/sequence\\n74:  * numbers from the original batch when the log is cleaned. This is required in order to be able to restore the\\n75:  * producer's state when the log is reloaded. If we did not retain the last sequence number, then following\\n76:  * a partition leader failure, once the new leader has rebuilt the producer state from the log, the next sequence\\n77:  * expected number would no longer be in sync with what was written by the client. This would cause an\\n78:  * unexpected OutOfOrderSequence error, which is typically fatal. The base sequence number must be preserved for\\n79:  * duplicate checking: the broker checks incoming Produce requests for duplicates by verifying that the first and\\n80:  * last sequence numbers of the incoming batch match the last from that producer.\\n81:  *\\n82:  * Note that if all of the records in a batch are removed during compaction, the broker may still retain an empty\\n83:  * batch header in order to preserve the producer sequence information as described above. These empty batches\\n84:  * are retained only until either a new sequence number is written by the corresponding producer or the producerId\\n85:  * is expired from lack of activity.\\n86:  *\\n87:  * There is no similar need to preserve the timestamp from the original batch after compaction. The BaseTimestamp\\n88:  * field therefore reflects the timestamp of the first record in the batch in most cases. If the batch is empty, the\\n89:  * BaseTimestamp will be set to -1 (NO_TIMESTAMP). If the delete horizon flag is set to 1, the BaseTimestamp\\n90:  * will be set to the time at which tombstone records and aborted transaction markers in the batch should be removed.\\n91:  *\\n92:  * Similarly, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the maximum timestamp of the current records if the timestamp type\\n93:  * is CREATE_TIME. For LOG_APPEND_TIME, on the other hand, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the timestamp set\\n94:  * by the broker and is preserved after compaction. Additionally, the MaxTimestamp of an empty batch always retains\\n95:  * the previous value prior to becoming empty.\\n96:  *\\n97:  * The current attributes are given below:\\n98:  *\\n99:  *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n100:  *  | Unused (7-15) | Delete Horizon Flag (6) | Control (5) | Transactional (4) | Timestamp Type (3) | Compression Type (0-2) |\\n101:  *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n102:  */\\n103: public class DefaultRecordBatch extends bufferSupplier, boolean skipKeyValue) {\\n280:         final InputStream inputStream = recordInputStream(bufferSupplier);\\n281: \\n282:         if (skipKeyValue) {\\n283:             return new See the NOTICE file distributed with\\n4:  * this work for additional information regarding copyright ownership.\\n5:  * The ASF licenses this file to You under the Apache License, Version 2.0\\n6:  * (the \\\"License\\\"); you may not use this file except in compliance with\\n7:  * the License. You may obtain a copy of the License at\\n8:  *\\n9:  *    http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0\\n10:  *\\n11:  * Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software\\n12:  * distributed under the License is distributed on an \\\"AS IS\\\" BASIS,\\n13:  * WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.\\n14:  * See the License for the specific language governing permissions and\\n15:  * limitations under the License.\\n16:  */\\n17: package org.apache.kafka.common.record.internal;\\n18: \\n19: import org.apache.kafka.common.InvalidRecordException;\\n20: import org.apache.kafka.common.KafkaException;\\n21: import org.apache.kafka.common.compress.Compression;\\n22: import org.apache.kafka.common.errors.CorruptRecordException;\\n23: import org.apache.kafka.common.header.Header;\\n24: import org.apache.kafka.common.record.TimestampType;\\n25: import org.apache.kafka.common.utils.BufferSupplier;\\n26: import org.apache.kafka.common.utils.ByteBufferOutputStream;\\n27: import org.apache.kafka.common.utils.ByteUtils;\\n28: import org.apache.kafka.common.utils.CloseableIterator;\\n29: import org.apache.kafka.common.utils.Crc32C;\\n30: \\n31: import java.io.IOException;\\n32: import java.io.InputStream;\\n33: import java.nio.BufferUnderflowException;\\n34: import java.nio.ByteBuffer;\\n35: import java.util.ArrayList;\\n36: import java.util.Collections;\\n37: import java.util.Iterator;\\n38: import java.util.List;\\n39: import java.util.NoSuchElementException;\\n40: import java.util.Objects;\\n41: import java.util.OptionalLong;\\n42: \\n43: import static org.apache.kafka.common.record.internal.Records.LOG_OVERHEAD;\\n44: \\n45: /**\\n46:  * RecordBatch implementation for magic 2 and above. The schema is given below:\\n47:  *\\n48:  * RecordBatch =>\\n49:  *  BaseOffset => Int64\\n50:  *  Length => Int32\\n51:  *  PartitionLeaderEpoch => Int32\\n52:  *  Magic => Int8\\n53:  *  CRC => Uint32\\n54:  *  Attributes => Int16\\n55:  *  LastOffsetDelta => Int32 // also serves as LastSequenceDelta\\n56:  *  BaseTimestamp => Int64\\n57:  *  MaxTimestamp => Int64\\n58:  *  ProducerId => Int64\\n59:  *  ProducerEpoch => Int16\\n60:  *  BaseSequence => Int32\\n61:  *  RecordsCount => Int32\\n62:  *  Records => [Record]\\n63:  *\\n64:  * Note that when compression is enabled (see attributes below), the compressed record data is serialized\\n65:  * directly following the count of the number of records.\\n66:  *\\n67:  * The CRC covers the data from the attributes to the end of the batch (i.e. all the bytes that follow the CRC). It is\\n68:  * located after the magic byte, which means that clients must parse the magic byte before deciding how to interpret\\n69:  * the bytes between the batch length and the magic byte. The partition leader epoch field is not included in the CRC\\n70:  * computation to avoid the need to recompute the CRC when this field is assigned for every batch that is received by\\n71:  * the broker. The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial is used for the computation.\\n72:  *\\n73:  * On Compaction: Unlike the older message formats, magic v2 and above preserves the first and last offset/sequence\\n74:  * numbers from the original batch when the log is cleaned. This is required in order to be able to restore the\\n75:  * producer's state when the log is reloaded. If we did not retain the last sequence number, then following\\n76:  * a partition leader failure, once the new leader has rebuilt the producer state from the log, the next sequence\\n77:  * expected number would no longer be in sync with what was written by the client. This would cause an\\n78:  * unexpected OutOfOrderSequence error, which is typically fatal. The base sequence number must be preserved for\\n79:  * duplicate checking: the broker checks incoming Produce requests for duplicates by verifying that the first and\\n80:  * last sequence numbers of the incoming batch match the last from that producer.\\n81:  *\\n82:  * Note that if all of the records in a batch are removed during compaction, the broker may still retain an empty\\n83:  * batch header in order to preserve the producer sequence information as described above. These empty batches\\n84:  * are retained only until either a new sequence number is written by the corresponding producer or the producerId\\n85:  * is expired from lack of activity.\\n86:  *\\n87:  * There is no similar need to preserve the timestamp from the original batch after compaction. The BaseTimestamp\\n88:  * field therefore reflects the timestamp of the first record in the batch in most cases. If the batch is empty, the\\n89:  * BaseTimestamp will be set to -1 (NO_TIMESTAMP). If the delete horizon flag is set to 1, the BaseTimestamp\\n90:  * will be set to the time at which tombstone records and aborted transaction markers in the batch should be removed.\\n91:  *\\n92:  * Similarly, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the maximum timestamp of the current records if the timestamp type\\n93:  * is CREATE_TIME. For LOG_APPEND_TIME, on the other hand, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the timestamp set\\n94:  * by the broker and is preserved after compaction. Additionally, the MaxTimestamp of an empty batch always retains\\n95:  * the previous value prior to becoming empty.\\n96:  *\\n97:  * The current attributes are given below:\\n98:  *\\n99:  *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n100:  *  | Unused (7-15) | Delete Horizon Flag (6) | Control (5) | Transactional (4) | Timestamp Type (3) | Compression Type (0-2) |\\n101:  *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n102:  */\\n103: public class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch implements MutableRecordBatch {\\n104:     static final int BASE_OFFSET_OFFSET = 0;\\n105:     static final int BASE_OFFSET_LENGTH = 8;\\n106:     static final int LENGTH_OFFSET = ATTRIBUTES_OFFSET);\\n401:     }\\n402: \\n403:     private byte attributes() {\\n404:         // note we're not using the second byte of attributes\\n405:         return (byte) buffer.getShort(ATTRIBUTES_OFFSET);\\n406:     }\\n407: \\n408:     @Override\\n409:     public boolean equals(Object o) {\\n410:         if (this == o)\\n411:             return true;\\n412:         if (o == null || getClass() != o.getClass())\\n413:             return false;\\n414: \\n415:         DefaultRecordBatch that = (DefaultRecordBatch) o;\\n416:         return Objects.equals(buffer, that.buffer);\\n417:     }\\n418: \\n419:     @Override\\n420:     public int hashCode() {\\n421:         return buffer != null ? buffer.hashCode() : 0;\\n422:     }\\n423: \\n424:     private static byte computeAttributes(CompressionType type, TimestampType timestampType,\\n425:                                           boolean isTransactional, boolean isControl, boolean isDeleteHorizonSet) {\\n426:         if (timestampType == TimestampType.NO_TIMESTAMP_TYPE)\\n427:             throw new IllegalArgumentException(\\\"Timestamp type must be provided to compute attributes for message \\\" +\\n428:                     \\\"format v2 and above\\\");\\n429: \\n430:         byte attributes = isTransactional ? See the NOTICE file distributed with\n4:  * this work for additional information regarding copyright ownership.\n5:  * The ASF licenses this file to You under the Apache License, Version 2.0\n6:  * (the \"License\"); you may not use this file except in compliance with\n7:  * the License. You may obtain a copy of the License at\n8:  *\n9:  *    http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0\n10:  *\n11:  * Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software\n12:  * distributed under the License is distributed on an \"AS IS\" BASIS,\n13:  * WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.\n14:  * See the License for the specific language governing permissions and\n15:  * limitations under the License.\n16:  */\n17: package org.apache.kafka.common.record.internal;\n18: \n19: import org.apache.kafka.common.InvalidRecordException;\n20: import org.apache.kafka.common.KafkaException;\n21: import org.apache.kafka.common.compress.Compression;\n22: import org.apache.kafka.common.errors.CorruptRecordException;\n23: import org.apache.kafka.common.header.Header;\n24: import org.apache.kafka.common.record.TimestampType;\n25: import org.apache.kafka.common.utils.BufferSupplier;\n26: import org.apache.kafka.common.utils.ByteBufferOutputStream;\n27: import org.apache.kafka.common.utils.ByteUtils;\n28: import org.apache.kafka.common.utils.CloseableIterator;\n29: import org.apache.kafka.common.utils.Crc32C;\n30: \n31: import java.io.IOException;\n32: import java.io.InputStream;\n33: import java.nio.BufferUnderflowException;\n34: import java.nio.ByteBuffer;\n35: import java.util.ArrayList;\n36: import java.util.Collections;\n37: import java.util.Iterator;\n38: import java.util.List;\n39: import java.util.NoSuchElementException;\n40: import java.util.Objects;\n41: import java.util.OptionalLong;\n42: \n43: import static org.apache.kafka.common.record.internal.Records.LOG_OVERHEAD;\n44: \n45: /**\n46:  * RecordBatch implementation for magic 2 and above. The schema is given below:\n47:  *\n48:  * RecordBatch =>\n49:  *  BaseOffset => Int64\n50:  *  Length => Int32\n51:  *  PartitionLeaderEpoch => Int32\n52:  *  Magic => Int8\n53:  *  CRC => Uint32\n54:  *  Attributes => Int16\n55:  *  LastOffsetDelta => Int32 // also serves as LastSequenceDelta\n56:  *  BaseTimestamp => Int64\n57:  *  MaxTimestamp => Int64\n58:  *  ProducerId => Int64\n59:  *  ProducerEpoch => Int16\n60:  *  BaseSequence => Int32\n61:  *  RecordsCount => Int32\n62:  *  Records => [Record]\n63:  *\n64:  * Note that when compression is enabled (see attributes below), the compressed record data is serialized\n65:  * directly following the count of the number of records.\n66:  *\n67:  * The CRC covers the data from the attributes to the end of the batch (i.e. all the bytes that follow the CRC). It is\n68:  * located after the magic byte, which means that clients must parse the magic byte before deciding how to interpret\n69:  * the bytes between the batch length and the magic byte. The partition leader epoch field is not included in the CRC\n70:  * computation to avoid the need to recompute the CRC when this field is assigned for every batch that is received by\n71:  * the broker. The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial is used for the computation.\n72:  *\n73:  * On Compaction: Unlike the older message formats, magic v2 and above preserves the first and last offset/sequence\n74:  * numbers from the original batch when the log is cleaned. This is required in order to be able to restore the\n75:  * producer's state when the log is reloaded. If we did not retain the last sequence number, then following\n76:  * a partition leader failure, once the new leader has rebuilt the producer state from the log, the next sequence\n77:  * expected number would no longer be in sync with what was written by the client. This would cause an\n78:  * unexpected OutOfOrderSequence error, which is typically fatal. The base sequence number must be preserved for\n79:  * duplicate checking: the broker checks incoming Produce requests for duplicates by verifying that the first and\n80:  * last sequence numbers of the incoming batch match the last from that producer.\n81:  *\n82:  * Note that if all of the records in a batch are removed during compaction, the broker may still retain an empty\n83:  * batch header in order to preserve the producer sequence information as described above. These empty batches\n84:  * are retained only until either a new sequence number is written by the corresponding producer or the producerId\n85:  * is expired from lack of activity.\n86:  *\n87:  * There is no similar need to preserve the timestamp from the original batch after compaction. The BaseTimestamp\n88:  * field therefore reflects the timestamp of the first record in the batch in most cases. If the batch is empty, the\n89:  * BaseTimestamp will be set to -1 (NO_TIMESTAMP). If the delete horizon flag is set to 1, the BaseTimestamp\n90:  * will be set to the time at which tombstone records and aborted transaction markers in the batch should be removed.\n91:  *\n92:  * Similarly, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the maximum timestamp of the current records if the timestamp type\n93:  * is CREATE_TIME. For LOG_APPEND_TIME, on the other hand, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the timestamp set\n94:  * by the broker and is preserved after compaction. Additionally, the MaxTimestamp of an empty batch always retains\n95:  * the previous value prior to becoming empty.\n96:  *\n97:  * The current attributes are given below:\n98:  *\n99:  *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------\n100:  *  | Unused (7-15) | Delete Horizon Flag (6) | Control (5) | Transactional (4) | Timestamp Type (3) | Compression Type (0-2) |\n101:  *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------\n102:  */\n103: public class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch Newer versions (magic\n * versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records regardless of compression.</p>\n *\n * <p><b>Thread Safety:</b> Implementations of this interface are <b>not thread-safe</b>. External\n * synchronization is required for concurrent access.</p>\n *\n * <p><b>Role in Kafka Protocol:</b> Record batches are fundamental to the Kafka wire protocol and\n * the commit log storage format. They provide efficient serialization of multiple records with\n * shared metadata (producer ID, epoch, timestamps, compression). The batch format enables features\n * like idempotent producers, transactional support, and efficient log compaction.</p>\n *\n * <p><b>Performance Characteristics:</b> For compressed batches, decompression is lazy—records are\n * decompressed on-demand via iterators. For uncompressed batches, record data is directly accessible\n * from the backing buffer without additional copying.</p>\n *\n * @see DefaultRecordBatch DefaultRecordBatch for magic v2+ implementation\n * @see MemoryRecords MemoryRecords for in-memory batch collections\n * @see Record Individual record in a batch\n */\npublic interface RecordBatch extends Iterable<Record> {"},"caller":{"type":"direct"}}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":10,"cache_creation_input_tokens":813,"cache_read_input_tokens":78923,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":813},"output_tokens":5,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"6dd5f166-9bea-4302-9242-4c3b5c46e336","uuid":"e9e3514e-a0f4-4f39-b91d-381bd8d40438"}

In old versions of the record format (versions 0 and 1),\n * a batch consisted always of a single record if no compression was enabled, but could contain\n * many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records\n * regardless of compression.\n */\npublic interface RecordBatch extends Iterable<Record> {","newString":"/**\n * A record batch is a container for records in the Kafka log format.\n *\n * <p>In old versions of the record format (versions 0 and 1), a batch consisted of a single record\n * if no compression was enabled, but could contain many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic\n * versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records regardless of compression.</p>\n *\n * <p><b>Thread Safety:</b> Implementations of this interface are <b>not thread-safe</b>. External\n * synchronization is required for concurrent access.</p>\n *\n * <p><b>Role in Kafka Protocol:</b> Record batches are fundamental to the Kafka wire protocol and\n * the commit log storage format. They provide efficient serialization of multiple records with\n * shared metadata (producer ID, epoch, timestamps, compression). The batch format enables features\n * like idempotent producers, transactional support, and efficient log compaction.</p>\n *\n * <p><b>Performance Characteristics:</b> For compressed batches, decompression is lazy—records are\n * decompressed on-demand via iterators. For uncompressed batches, record data is directly accessible\n * from the backing buffer without additional copying.</p>\n *\n * @see DefaultRecordBatch DefaultRecordBatch for magic v2+ implementation\n * @see MemoryRecords MemoryRecords for in-memory batch collections\n * @see Record Individual record in a batch\n */\npublic interface RecordBatch extends Iterable<Record> {","originalFile":"/*\n * Licensed to the Apache Software Foundation (ASF) under one or more\n * contributor license agreements. See the NOTICE file distributed with\n * this work for additional information regarding copyright ownership.\n * The ASF licenses this file to You under the Apache License, Version 2.0\n * (the \"License\"); you may not use this file except in compliance with\n * the License. You may obtain a copy of the License at\n *\n *    http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0\n *\n * Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software\n * distributed under the License is distributed on an \"AS IS\" BASIS,\n * WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.\n * See the License for the specific language governing permissions and\n * limitations under the License.\n */\npackage org.apache.kafka.common.record.internal;\n\nimport org.apache.kafka.common.record.TimestampType;\nimport org.apache.kafka.common.utils.BufferSupplier;\nimport org.apache.kafka.common.utils.CloseableIterator;\n\nimport java.nio.ByteBuffer;\nimport java.util.Iterator;\nimport java.util.Optional;\nimport java.util.OptionalLong;\n\n/**\n * A record batch is a container for records. In old versions of the record format (versions 0 and 1),\n * a batch consisted always of a single record if no compression was enabled, but could contain\n * many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records\n * regardless of compression.\n */\npublic interface RecordBatch extends Iterable<Record> {\n\n    /**\n     * The \"magic\" values\n     */\n    byte MAGIC_VALUE_V0 = 0;\n    byte MAGIC_VALUE_V1 = 1;\n    byte MAGIC_VALUE_V2 = 2;\n\n    /**\n     * The current \"magic\" value\n     */\n    byte CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE = MAGIC_VALUE_V2;\n\n    /**\n     * Timestamp value for records without a timestamp\n     */\n    long NO_TIMESTAMP = -1L;\n\n    /**\n     * Values used in the v2 record format by non-idempotent/non-transactional producers or when\n     * up-converting from an older format.\n     */\n    long NO_PRODUCER_ID = -1L;\n    short NO_PRODUCER_EPOCH = -1;\n    int NO_SEQUENCE = -1;\n\n    /**\n     * Used to indicate an unknown leader epoch, which will be the case when the record set is\n     * first created by the producer.\n     */\n    int NO_PARTITION_LEADER_EPOCH = -1;\n\n    /**\n     * Check whether the checksum of this batch is correct.\n     *\n     * @return true If so, false otherwise\n     */\n    boolean isValid();\n\n    /**\n     * Raise an exception if the checksum is not valid.\n     */\n    void ensureValid();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the checksum of this record batch, which covers the batch header as well as all of the records.\n     *\n     * @return The 4-byte unsigned checksum represented as a long\n     */\n    long checksum();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the max timestamp or log append time of this record batch.\n     *\n     * If the timestamp type is create time, this is the max timestamp among all records contained in this batch and\n     * the value is updated during compaction.\n     *\n     * @return The max timestamp\n     */\n    long maxTimestamp();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the timestamp type of this record batch. This will be {@link TimestampType#NO_TIMESTAMP_TYPE}\n     * if the batch has magic 0.\n     *\n     * @return The timestamp type\n     */\n    TimestampType timestampType();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the base offset contained in this record batch. For magic version prior to 2, the base offset will\n     * always be the offset of the first message in the batch. This generally requires deep iteration and will\n     * return the offset of the first record in the record batch. For magic version 2 and above, this will return\n     * the first offset of the original record batch (i.e. prior to compaction). For non-compacted topics, the\n     * behavior is equivalent.\n     *\n     * Because this requires deep iteration for older magic versions, this method should be used with\n     * caution. Generally {@link #lastOffset()} is safer since access is efficient for all magic versions.\n     *\n     * @return The base offset of this record batch (which may or may not be the offset of the first record\n     *         as described above).\n     */\n    long baseOffset();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the last offset in this record batch (inclusive). Just like {@link #baseOffset()}, the last offset\n     * always reflects the offset of the last record in the original batch, even if it is removed during log\n     * compaction.\n     *\n     * @return The offset of the last record in this batch\n     */\n    long lastOffset();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the offset following this record batch (i.e. the last offset contained in this batch plus one).\n     *\n     * @return the next consecutive offset following this batch\n     */\n    long nextOffset();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the record format version of this record batch (i.e its magic value).\n     *\n     * @return the magic byte\n     */\n    byte magic();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the producer id for this log record batch. For older magic versions, this will return -1.\n     *\n     * @return The producer id or -1 if there is none\n     */\n    long producerId();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the producer epoch for this log record batch.\n     *\n     * @return The producer epoch, or -1 if there is none\n     */\n    short producerEpoch();\n\n    /**\n     * Does the batch have a valid producer id set.\n     */\n    boolean hasProducerId();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the base sequence number of this record batch. Like {@link #baseOffset()}, this value is not\n     * affected by compaction: it always retains the base sequence number from the original batch.\n     *\n     * @return The first sequence number or -1 if there is none\n     */\n    int baseSequence();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the last sequence number of this record batch. Like {@link #lastOffset()}, the last sequence number\n     * always reflects the sequence number of the last record in the original batch, even if it is removed during log\n     * compaction.\n     *\n     * @return The last sequence number or -1 if there is none\n     */\n    int lastSequence();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the compression type of this record batch.\n     *\n     * @return The compression type\n     */\n    CompressionType compressionType();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the size in bytes of this batch, including the size of the record and the batch overhead.\n     * @return The size in bytes of this batch\n     */\n    int sizeInBytes();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the count if it is efficiently supported by the record format (which is only the case\n     * for magic 2 and higher).\n     *\n     * @return The number of records in the batch or null for magic versions 0 and 1.\n     */\n    Integer countOrNull();\n\n    /**\n     * Check whether this record batch is compressed.\n     * @return true if so, false otherwise\n     */\n    boolean isCompressed();\n\n    /**\n     * Write this record batch into a buffer.\n     * @param buffer The buffer to write the batch to\n     */\n    void writeTo(ByteBuffer buffer);\n\n    /**\n     * Whether or not this record batch is part of a transaction.\n     * @return true if it is, false otherwise\n     */\n    boolean isTransactional();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the delete horizon, returns OptionalLong.EMPTY if the first timestamp is not the delete horizon\n     * @return timestamp of the delete horizon\n     */\n    OptionalLong deleteHorizonMs();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the partition leader epoch of this record batch.\n     * @return The leader epoch or -1 if it is unknown\n     */\n    int partitionLeaderEpoch();\n\n    /**\n     * Return a streaming iterator which basically delays decompression of the record stream until the records\n     * are actually asked for using {@link Iterator#next()}. If the message format does not support streaming\n     * iteration, then the normal iterator is returned. Either way, callers should ensure that the iterator is closed.\n     *\n     * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression if supported.\n     *                                    For small record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4)\n     *                                    will dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating over the records in the\n     *                                    batch. As such, a supplier that reuses buffers will have a significant\n     *                                    performance impact.\n     * @return The closeable iterator\n     */\n    CloseableIterator<Record> streamingIterator(BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier);\n\n    /**\n     * Check whether this is a control batch (i.e. whether the control bit is set in the batch attributes).\n     * For magic versions prior to 2, this is always false.\n     *\n     * @return Whether this is a batch containing control records\n     */\n    boolean isControlBatch();\n\n    /**\n     * iterate all records to find the offset of max timestamp.\n     * noted:\n     * 1) that the earliest offset will return if there are multi records having same (max) timestamp\n     * 2) it always returns None if the {@link RecordBatch#magic()} is equal to {@link RecordBatch#MAGIC_VALUE_V0}\n     * @return offset of max timestamp\n     */\n    default Optional<Long> offsetOfMaxTimestamp() {\n        if (magic() == RecordBatch.MAGIC_VALUE_V0) return Optional.empty();\n        long maxTimestamp = maxTimestamp();\n        try (CloseableIterator<Record> iter = streamingIterator(BufferSupplier.create())) {\n            while (iter.hasNext()) {\n                Record record = iter.next();\n                if (maxTimestamp == record.timestamp()) return Optional.of(record.offset());\n            }\n        }\n        return Optional.empty();\n    }\n}\n","structuredPatch":[{"oldStart":26,"oldLines":10,"newStart":26,"newLines":27,"lines":[" import java.util.OptionalLong;"," "," /**","- * A record batch is a container for records. Newer versions (magic\n * versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records regardless of compression.</p>\n *\n * <p><b>Thread Safety:</b> Implementations of this interface are <b>not thread-safe</b>. External\n * synchronization is required for concurrent access.</p>\n *\n * <p><b>Role in Kafka Protocol:</b> Record batches are fundamental to the Kafka wire protocol and\n * the commit log storage format. They provide efficient serialization of multiple records with\n * shared metadata (producer ID, epoch, timestamps, compression). The batch format enables features\n * like idempotent producers, transactional support, and efficient log compaction.</p>\n *\n * <p><b>Performance Characteristics:</b> For compressed batches, decompression is lazy—records are\n * decompressed on-demand via iterators. For uncompressed batches, record data is directly accessible\n * from the backing buffer without additional copying.</p>\n *\n * @see DefaultRecordBatch DefaultRecordBatch for magic v2+ implementation\n * @see MemoryRecords MemoryRecords for in-memory batch collections\n * @see Record Individual record in a batch\n */\npublic interface RecordBatch extends Iterable<Record> {\n\n For magic version prior to 2, the base offset will\n     * always be the offset of the first message in the batch. This generally requires deep iteration and will\n     * return the offset of the first record in the record batch. For magic version 2 and above, this will return\n     * the first offset of the original record batch (i.e. prior to compaction). For non-compacted topics, the\n     * behavior is equivalent.\n     *\n     * Because this requires deep iteration for older magic versions, this method should be used with\n     * caution. Generally {@link #lastOffset()} is safer since access is efficient for all magic versions.\n     *\n     * @return The base offset of this record batch (which may or may not be the offset of the first record\n     *         as described above).\n     */\n    long baseOffset();","newString":"    /**\n     * Get the base offset contained in this record batch.\n     *\n     * <p>For magic version prior to 2, this returns the offset of the first record in the batch,\n     * which generally requires deep iteration. For magic version 2 and above, this returns the\n     * first offset of the original record batch (prior to compaction), which is stored directly in\n     * the batch header and is efficiently accessible. For non-compacted topics, the behavior is\n     * equivalent across versions.</p>\n     *\n     * <p><b>Performance Note:</b> For older magic versions (v0, v1), this method requires iterating\n     * through records and should be used with caution. {@link #lastOffset()} is safer and more\n     * efficient for all magic versions.</p>\n     *\n     * @return the base offset of this record batch\n     * @see #lastOffset() for the more efficiently accessible last offset\n     */\n    long baseOffset();","originalFile":"/*\n * Licensed to the Apache Software Foundation (ASF) under one or more\n * contributor license agreements. See the NOTICE file distributed with\n * this work for additional information regarding copyright ownership.\n * The ASF licenses this file to You under the Apache License, Version 2.0\n * (the \"License\"); you may not use this file except in compliance with\n * the License. You may obtain a copy of the License at\n *\n *    http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0\n *\n * Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software\n * distributed under the License is distributed on an \"AS IS\" BASIS,\n * WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.\n * See the License for the specific language governing permissions and\n * limitations under the License.\n */\npackage org.apache.kafka.common.record.internal;\n\nimport org.apache.kafka.common.record.TimestampType;\nimport org.apache.kafka.common.utils.BufferSupplier;\nimport org.apache.kafka.common.utils.CloseableIterator;\n\nimport java.nio.ByteBuffer;\nimport java.util.Iterator;\nimport java.util.Optional;\nimport java.util.OptionalLong;\n\n/**\n * A record batch is a container for records in the Kafka log format.\n *\n * <p>In old versions of the record format (versions 0 and 1), a batch consisted of a single record\n * if no compression was enabled, but could contain many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic\n * versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records regardless of compression.</p>\n *\n * <p><b>Thread Safety:</b> Implementations of this interface are <b>not thread-safe</b>. External\n * synchronization is required for concurrent access.</p>\n *\n * <p><b>Role in Kafka Protocol:</b> Record batches are fundamental to the Kafka wire protocol and\n * the commit log storage format. They provide efficient serialization of multiple records with\n * shared metadata (producer ID, epoch, timestamps, compression). The batch format enables features\n * like idempotent producers, transactional support, and efficient log compaction.</p>\n *\n * <p><b>Performance Characteristics:</b> For compressed batches, decompression is lazy—records are\n * decompressed on-demand via iterators. For uncompressed batches, record data is directly accessible\n * from the backing buffer without additional copying.</p>\n *\n * @see DefaultRecordBatch DefaultRecordBatch for magic v2+ implementation\n * @see MemoryRecords MemoryRecords for in-memory batch collections\n * @see Record Individual record in a batch\n */\npublic interface RecordBatch extends Iterable<Record> {\n\n    /**\n     * The \"magic\" values\n     */\n    byte MAGIC_VALUE_V0 = 0;\n    byte MAGIC_VALUE_V1 = 1;\n    byte MAGIC_VALUE_V2 = 2;\n\n    /**\n     * The current \"magic\" value\n     */\n    byte CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE = MAGIC_VALUE_V2;\n\n    /**\n     * Timestamp value for records without a timestamp\n     */\n    long NO_TIMESTAMP = -1L;\n\n    /**\n     * Values used in the v2 record format by non-idempotent/non-transactional producers or when\n     * up-converting from an older format.\n     */\n    long NO_PRODUCER_ID = -1L;\n    short NO_PRODUCER_EPOCH = -1;\n    int NO_SEQUENCE = -1;\n\n    /**\n     * Used to indicate an unknown leader epoch, which will be the case when the record set is\n     * first created by the producer.\n     */\n    int NO_PARTITION_LEADER_EPOCH = -1;\n\n    /**\n     * Check whether the CRC-32C checksum of this batch is correct.\n     *\n     * <p>The checksum covers the batch header (from attributes byte onwards) and all record data.\n     * The partition leader epoch field is excluded from CRC computation to avoid recomputation\n     * when the field is assigned by the broker.</p>\n     *\n     * @return true if the computed checksum matches the stored checksum, false otherwise\n     * @see #checksum() to retrieve the stored checksum value\n     */\n    boolean isValid();\n\n    /**\n     * Raise an exception if the checksum of this batch is not valid.\n     *\n     * @throws CorruptRecordException if the stored checksum does not match the computed checksum\n     * @see #isValid() for non-exceptional validation\n     */\n    void ensureValid();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the checksum of this record batch, which covers the batch header as well as all of the records.\n     *\n     * @return The 4-byte unsigned checksum represented as a long\n     */\n    long checksum();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the max timestamp or log append time of this record batch.\n     *\n     * If the timestamp type is create time, this is the max timestamp among all records contained in this batch and\n     * the value is updated during compaction.\n     *\n     * @return The max timestamp\n     */\n    long maxTimestamp();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the timestamp type of this record batch. This will be {@link TimestampType#NO_TIMESTAMP_TYPE}\n     * if the batch has magic 0.\n     *\n     * @return The timestamp type\n     */\n    TimestampType timestampType();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the base offset contained in this record batch. For magic version prior to 2, the base offset will\n     * always be the offset of the first message in the batch. This generally requires deep iteration and will\n     * return the offset of the first record in the record batch. For magic version 2 and above, this will return\n     * the first offset of the original record batch (i.e. prior to compaction). For non-compacted topics, the\n     * behavior is equivalent.\n     *\n     * Because this requires deep iteration for older magic versions, this method should be used with\n     * caution. Generally {@link #lastOffset()} is safer since access is efficient for all magic versions.\n     *\n     * @return The base offset of this record batch (which may or may not be the offset of the first record\n     *         as described above).\n     */\n    long baseOffset();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the last offset in this record batch (inclusive). Just like {@link #baseOffset()}, the last offset\n     * always reflects the offset of the last record in the original batch, even if it is removed during log\n     * compaction.\n     *\n     * @return The offset of the last record in this batch\n     */\n    long lastOffset();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the offset following this record batch (i.e. the last offset contained in this batch plus one).\n     *\n     * @return the next consecutive offset following this batch\n     */\n    long nextOffset();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the record format version of this record batch (i.e its magic value).\n     *\n     * @return the magic byte\n     */\n    byte magic();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the producer id for this log record batch. For older magic versions, this will return -1.\n     *\n     * @return The producer id or -1 if there is none\n     */\n    long producerId();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the producer epoch for this log record batch.\n     *\n     * @return The producer epoch, or -1 if there is none\n     */\n    short producerEpoch();\n\n    /**\n     * Does the batch have a valid producer id set.\n     */\n    boolean hasProducerId();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the base sequence number of this record batch. Like {@link #baseOffset()}, this value is not\n     * affected by compaction: it always retains the base sequence number from the original batch.\n     *\n     * @return The first sequence number or -1 if there is none\n     */\n    int baseSequence();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the last sequence number of this record batch. Like {@link #lastOffset()}, the last sequence number\n     * always reflects the sequence number of the last record in the original batch, even if it is removed during log\n     * compaction.\n     *\n     * @return The last sequence number or -1 if there is none\n     */\n    int lastSequence();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the compression type of this record batch.\n     *\n     * @return The compression type\n     */\n    CompressionType compressionType();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the size in bytes of this batch, including the size of the record and the batch overhead.\n     * @return The size in bytes of this batch\n     */\n    int sizeInBytes();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the count if it is efficiently supported by the record format (which is only the case\n     * for magic 2 and higher).\n     *\n     * @return The number of records in the batch or null for magic versions 0 and 1.\n     */\n    Integer countOrNull();\n\n    /**\n     * Check whether this record batch is compressed.\n     * @return true if so, false otherwise\n     */\n    boolean isCompressed();\n\n    /**\n     * Write this record batch into a buffer.\n     * @param buffer The buffer to write the batch to\n     */\n    void writeTo(ByteBuffer buffer);\n\n    /**\n     * Whether or not this record batch is part of a transaction.\n     * @return true if it is, false otherwise\n     */\n    boolean isTransactional();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the delete horizon, returns OptionalLong.EMPTY if the first timestamp is not the delete horizon\n     * @return timestamp of the delete horizon\n     */\n    OptionalLong deleteHorizonMs();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the partition leader epoch of this record batch.\n     * @return The leader epoch or -1 if it is unknown\n     */\n    int partitionLeaderEpoch();\n\n    /**\n     * Return a streaming iterator which basically delays decompression of the record stream until the records\n     * are actually asked for using {@link Iterator#next()}. If the message format does not support streaming\n     * iteration, then the normal iterator is returned. Either way, callers should ensure that the iterator is closed.\n     *\n     * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression if supported.\n     *                                    For small record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4)\n     *                                    will dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating over the records in the\n     *                                    batch. As such, a supplier that reuses buffers will have a significant\n     *                                    performance impact.\n     * @return The closeable iterator\n     */\n    CloseableIterator<Record> streamingIterator(BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier);\n\n    /**\n     * Check whether this is a control batch (i.e. whether the control bit is set in the batch attributes).\n     * For magic versions prior to 2, this is always false.\n     *\n     * @return Whether this is a batch containing control records\n     */\n    boolean isControlBatch();\n\n    /**\n     * iterate all records to find the offset of max timestamp.\n     * noted:\n     * 1) that the earliest offset will return if there are multi records having same (max) timestamp\n     * 2) it always returns None if the {@link RecordBatch#magic()} is equal to {@link RecordBatch#MAGIC_VALUE_V0}\n     * @return offset of max timestamp\n     */\n    default Optional<Long> offsetOfMaxTimestamp() {\n        if (magic() == RecordBatch.MAGIC_VALUE_V0) return Optional.empty();\n        long maxTimestamp = maxTimestamp();\n        try (CloseableIterator<Record> iter = streamingIterator(BufferSupplier.create())) {\n            while (iter.hasNext()) {\n                Record record = iter.next();\n                if (maxTimestamp == record.timestamp()) return Optional.of(record.offset());\n            }\n        }\n        return Optional.empty();\n    }\n}\n","structuredPatch":[{"oldStart":127,"oldLines":17,"newStart":127,"newLines":20,"lines":["     TimestampType timestampType();"," ","     /**","-     * Get the base offset contained in this record batch. This method enables memory-efficient processing of\n     * compressed batches by decompressing records on-demand rather than upfront.</p>\n     *\n     * <p><b>Performance Characteristics:</b> For compressed batches, this avoids allocating large\n     * decompression buffers upfront. For small record batches, the cost of allocating a buffer\n     * (e.g., 64 KB for LZ4) can dominate decompression cost. Using a {@code decompressionBufferSupplier}\n     * that reuses buffers across multiple batches can significantly reduce allocation overhead.</p>\n     *\n     * @param decompressionBufferSupplier supplier of ByteBuffer(s) for decompression. For older magic versions, this will return -1.\n     *\n     * @return The producer id or -1 if there is none\n     */\n    long producerId();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the producer epoch for this log record batch.\n     *\n     * @return The producer epoch, or -1 if there is none\n     */\n    short producerEpoch();\n\n    /**\n     * Does the batch have a valid producer id set.\n     */\n    boolean hasProducerId();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the base sequence number of this record batch. Like {@link #lastOffset()}, the last sequence number\n     * always reflects the sequence number of the last record in the original batch, even if it is removed during log\n     * compaction.\n     *\n     * @return The last sequence number or -1 if there is none\n     */\n    int lastSequence();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the compression type of this record batch.\n     *\n     * @return The compression type\n     */\n    CompressionType compressionType();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the size in bytes of this batch, including the size of the record and the batch overhead.\n     * @return The size in bytes of this batch\n     */\n    int sizeInBytes();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the count if it is efficiently supported by the record format (which is only the case\n     * for magic 2 and higher).\n     *\n     * @return The number of records in the batch or null for magic versions 0 and 1.\n     */\n    Integer countOrNull();\n\n    /**\n     * Check whether this record batch is compressed.\n     * @return true if so, false otherwise\n     */\n    boolean isCompressed();\n\n    /**\n     * Write this record batch into a buffer.\n     * @param buffer The buffer to write the batch to\n     */\n    void writeTo(ByteBuffer buffer);\n\n    /**\n     * Whether or not this record batch is part of a transaction.\n     * @return true if it is, false otherwise\n     */\n    boolean isTransactional();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the delete horizon, returns OptionalLong.EMPTY if the first timestamp is not the delete horizon\n     * @return timestamp of the delete horizon\n     */\n    OptionalLong deleteHorizonMs();\n\n    /**\n     * Get the partition leader epoch of this record batch.\n     * @return The leader epoch or -1 if it is unknown\n     */\n    int partitionLeaderEpoch();\n\n    /**\n     * Return a streaming iterator which basically delays decompression of the record stream until the records\n     * are actually asked for using {@link Iterator#next()}. If the message format does not support streaming\n     * iteration, then the normal iterator is returned. Either way, callers should ensure that the iterator is closed.\n     *\n     * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression if supported.\n     *                                    For small record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4)\n     *                                    will dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating over the records in the\n     *                                    batch. As such, a supplier that reuses buffers will have a significant\n     *                                    performance impact.\n     * @return The closeable iterator\n     */\n    CloseableIterator<Record> streamingIterator(BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier);\n\n    /**\n     * Check whether this is a control batch (i.e. whether the control bit is set in the batch attributes).\n     * For magic versions prior to 2, this is always false.\n     *\n     * @return Whether this is a batch containing control records\n     */\n    boolean isControlBatch();\n\n    /**\n     * iterate all records to find the offset of max timestamp.\n     * noted:\n     * 1) that the earliest offset will return if there are multi records having same (max) timestamp\n     * 2) it always returns None if the {@link RecordBatch#magic()} is equal to {@link RecordBatch#MAGIC_VALUE_V0}\n     * @return offset of max timestamp\n     */\n    default Optional<Long> offsetOfMaxTimestamp() {\n        if (magic() == RecordBatch.MAGIC_VALUE_V0) return Optional.empty();\n        long maxTimestamp = maxTimestamp();\n        try (CloseableIterator<Record> iter = streamingIterator(BufferSupplier.create())) {\n            while (iter.hasNext()) {\n                Record record = iter.next();\n                if (maxTimestamp == record.timestamp()) return Optional.of(record.offset());\n            }\n        }\n        return Optional.empty();\n    }\n}\n","structuredPatch":[{"oldStart":255,"oldLines":16,"newStart":255,"newLines":26,"lines":["     int partitionLeaderEpoch();"," ","     /**","-     * Return a streaming iterator which basically delays decompression of the record stream until the records","-     * are actually asked for using {@link Iterator#next()}. The schema is given below:\n    47→ *\n    48→ * RecordBatch =>\n    49→ *  BaseOffset => Int64\n    50→ *  Length => Int32\n    51→ *  PartitionLeaderEpoch => Int32\n    52→ *  Magic => Int8\n    53→ *  CRC => Uint32\n    54→ *  Attributes => Int16\n    55→ *  LastOffsetDelta => Int32 // also serves as LastSequenceDelta\n    56→ *  BaseTimestamp => Int64\n    57→ *  MaxTimestamp => Int64\n    58→ *  ProducerId => Int64\n    59→ *  ProducerEpoch => Int16\n    60→ *  BaseSequence => Int32\n    61→ *  RecordsCount => Int32\n    62→ *  Records => [Record]\n    63→ *\n    64→ * Note that when compression is enabled (see attributes below), the compressed record data is serialized\n    65→ * directly following the count of the number of records.\n    66→ *\n    67→ * The CRC covers the data from the attributes to the end of the batch (i.e. all the bytes that follow the CRC). It is\n    68→ * located after the magic byte, which means that clients must parse the magic byte before deciding how to interpret\n    69→ * the bytes between the batch length and the magic byte. The partition leader epoch field is not included in the CRC\n    70→ * computation to avoid the need to recompute the CRC when this field is assigned for every batch that is received by\n    71→ * the broker. The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial is used for the computation.\n    72→ *\n    73→ * On Compaction: Unlike the older message formats, magic v2 and above preserves the first and last offset/sequence\n    74→ * numbers from the original batch when the log is cleaned. This is required in order to be able to restore the\n    75→ * producer's state when the log is reloaded. If we did not retain the last sequence number, then following\n    76→ * a partition leader failure, once the new leader has rebuilt the producer state from the log, the next sequence\n    77→ * expected number would no longer be in sync with what was written by the client. This would cause an\n    78→ * unexpected OutOfOrderSequence error, which is typically fatal. The base sequence number must be preserved for\n    79→ * duplicate checking: the broker checks incoming Produce requests for duplicates by verifying that the first and\n    80→ * last sequence numbers of the incoming batch match the last from that producer.\n    81→ *\n    82→ * Note that if all of the records in a batch are removed during compaction, the broker may still retain an empty\n    83→ * batch header in order to preserve the producer sequence information as described above. These empty batches\n    84→ * are retained only until either a new sequence number is written by the corresponding producer or the producerId\n    85→ * is expired from lack of activity.\n    86→ *\n    87→ * There is no similar need to preserve the timestamp from the original batch after compaction. The BaseTimestamp\n    88→ * field therefore reflects the timestamp of the first record in the batch in most cases. If the batch is empty, the\n    89→ * BaseTimestamp will be set to -1 (NO_TIMESTAMP). If the delete horizon flag is set to 1, the BaseTimestamp\n    90→ * will be set to the time at which tombstone records and aborted transaction markers in the batch should be removed.\n    91→ *\n    92→ * Similarly, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the maximum timestamp of the current records if the timestamp type\n    93→ * is CREATE_TIME. For LOG_APPEND_TIME, on the other hand, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the timestamp set\n    94→ * by the broker and is preserved after compaction. Additionally, the MaxTimestamp of an empty batch always retains\n    95→ * the previous value prior to becoming empty.\n    96→ *\n    97→ * The current attributes are given below:\n    98→ *\n    99→ *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------\n   100→ *  | Unused (7-15) | Delete Horizon Flag (6) | Control (5) | Transactional (4) | Timestamp Type (3) | Compression Type (0-2) |\n   101→ *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------\n   102→ */\n   103→public class DefaultRecordBatch extends {"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_01BTUTtKDB1SLrLMUajw2936","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"tool_use","id":"toolu_01BXNKYyX4fv5GzVz8yD5zU1","name":"Edit","input":{"replace_all":false,"file_path":"/workspace/kafka-repo/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java","old_string":"/**\n * RecordBatch implementation for magic 2 and above. The schema is given below:\n *\n * RecordBatch =>\n *  BaseOffset => Int64\n *  Length => Int32\n *  PartitionLeaderEpoch => Int32\n *  Magic => Int8\n *  CRC => Uint32\n *  Attributes => Int16\n *  LastOffsetDelta => Int32 // also serves as LastSequenceDelta\n *  BaseTimestamp => Int64\n *  MaxTimestamp => Int64\n *  ProducerId => Int64\n *  ProducerEpoch => Int16\n *  BaseSequence => Int32\n *  RecordsCount => Int32\n *  Records => [Record]\n *\n * Note that when compression is enabled (see attributes below), the compressed record data is serialized\n * directly following the count of the number of records.\n *\n * The CRC covers the data from the attributes to the end of the batch (i.e. all the bytes that follow the CRC). It is\n * located after the magic byte, which means that clients must parse the magic byte before deciding how to interpret\n * the bytes between the batch length and the magic byte. The partition leader epoch field is not included in the CRC\n * computation to avoid the need to recompute the CRC when this field is assigned for every batch that is received by\n * the broker. The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial is used for the computation.\n *\n * On Compaction: Unlike the older message formats, magic v2 and above preserves the first and last offset/sequence\n * numbers from the original batch when the log is cleaned. This is required in order to be able to restore the\n * producer's state when the log is reloaded. If we did not retain the last sequence number, then following\n * a partition leader failure, once the new leader has rebuilt the producer state from the log, the next sequence\n * expected number would no longer be in sync with what was written by the client. This would cause an\n * unexpected OutOfOrderSequence error, which is typically fatal. The base sequence number must be preserved for\n * duplicate checking: the broker checks incoming Produce requests for duplicates by verifying that the first and\n * last sequence numbers of the incoming batch match the last from that producer.\n *\n * Note that if all of the records in a batch are removed during compaction, the broker may still retain an empty\n * batch header in order to preserve the producer sequence information as described above. These empty batches\n * are retained only until either a new sequence number is written by the corresponding producer or the producerId\n * is expired from lack of activity.\n *\n * There is no similar need to preserve the timestamp from the original batch after compaction. The BaseTimestamp\n * field therefore reflects the timestamp of the first record in the batch in most cases. If the batch is empty, the\n * BaseTimestamp will be set to -1 (NO_TIMESTAMP). If the delete horizon flag is set to 1, the BaseTimestamp\n * will be set to the time at which tombstone records and aborted transaction markers in the batch should be removed.\n *\n * Similarly, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the maximum timestamp of the current records if the timestamp type\n * is CREATE_TIME. For LOG_APPEND_TIME, on the other hand, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the timestamp set\n * by the broker and is preserved after compaction. Additionally, the MaxTimestamp of an empty batch always retains\n * the previous value prior to becoming empty.\n *\n * The current attributes are given below:\n *\n *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------\n *  | Unused (7-15) | Delete Horizon Flag (6) | Control (5) | Transactional (4) | Timestamp Type (3) | Compression Type (0-2) |\n *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------\n */\npublic class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch implements MutableRecordBatch {","new_string":"/**\n * Concrete implementation of {@link RecordBatch} for magic version 2 and above.\n *\n * <p><b>Binary Format:</b> Records in magic v2+ follow the schema below:\n * <pre>\n * RecordBatch =>\n *  BaseOffset => Int64\n *  Length => Int32\n *  PartitionLeaderEpoch => Int32\n *  Magic => Int8\n *  CRC => Uint32 (CRC-32C from attributes to end of batch)\n *  Attributes => Int16\n *  LastOffsetDelta => Int32\n *  BaseTimestamp => Int64 (timestamp of first record, or delete horizon if flag set)\n *  MaxTimestamp => Int64\n *  ProducerId => Int64 (or -1 if not set)\n *  ProducerEpoch => Int16\n *  BaseSequence => Int32\n *  RecordsCount => Int32\n *  Records => [Record]\n * </pre>\n * When compression is enabled, compressed record data follows directly after the count field.</p>\n *\n * <p><b>Thread Safety:</b> This class is <b>not thread-safe</b>. Batch state can be modified in-place\n * (e.g., via {@link #setMaxTimestamp(TimestampType, long)}) and must be externally synchronized for\n * concurrent access.</p>\n *\n * <p><b>CRC Computation:</b> The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial covers all bytes from the attributes\n * field through the end of the batch. The partition leader epoch field is excluded to avoid CRC\n * recomputation when assigned by the broker. The magic byte must be parsed before interpreting bytes\n * between batch length and CRC.</p>\n *\n * <p><b>Log Compaction Semantics:</b> Magic v2+ preserves the first and last offset/sequence numbers\n * from the original batch during log cleaning. This ensures producer state can be correctly restored\n * after leader failover. Base sequence is needed for duplicate detection. Empty batches may be retained\n * to preserve producer state until a new sequence is written or the producer ID expires.</p>\n *\n * <p><b>Performance Characteristics:</b>\n * <ul>\n *   <li>Header fields (offsets, timestamps, producer ID) are efficiently accessible directly from the\n *       buffer without iteration.</li>\n *   <li>For compressed batches, records are decompressed on-demand via iterators.</li>\n *   <li>Record count is efficiently accessible via {@link #countOrNull()}.</li>\n * </ul></p>\n *\n * @see MemoryRecords MemoryRecords for creating/managing batches in memory\n * @see RecordBatch base interface\n */\npublic class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch implements MutableRecordBatch {"},"caller":{"type":"direct"}}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":6,"cache_creation_input_tokens":3081,"cache_read_input_tokens":84707,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":3081},"output_tokens":1,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"6dd5f166-9bea-4302-9242-4c3b5c46e336","uuid":"fda2731e-f234-4d16-9609-b30467d5246b"}
